Outstanding Biological Questions

Current outstanding questions in molecular cell biology

One overarching problem biology now confronts is predicting the future behavior of biological systems given knowledge of their present state. The fact that we can even articulate and propose to address this problem is a testimony to the success of 20th century molecular and cellular biology. However, consideration of the big problem (prediction) reveals other, intermediate problems that biology has not so far addressed but that we will face during work on the Alpa Project. These include, but are not limited to:

a) The extent, sources, and importance of epigenetic variation. As mentioned before, behavioral variation among different members of genetically identical populations has long been known to occur, and has been attributed to the stochastic fluctuations in numbers of molecular species. Speaking generally, there is no prokaryotic or eukaryotic biological system where the extent of such variation in system output or in any intermediate step is well characterized, or its consequences well understood. For example, we know that numerous cancers arise due to mutations in clones of somatic cells, but we do not know whether any of the molecular events that cause one cell to progress and another not might be consequences of epigenetic variation in regulatory molecules.

b) The utility of extending the range of observed phenomena to include quantitative and dynamic effects. To be fair, a good deal of genetics, particularly in agriculture, since the early 20th century has concerned itself with quantitative behavior (Saxe, 1923), and the revitalization of human genetics contributed to the widespread realization that the study of quantitative traits would be important for humans (Lander & Botstein, 1989). Moreover, it is now appreciated that some of the most important human phenotypic traits of interest to medicine under genomic study will be quantitative (Cantor, personal communication). However, inspection of any biological journal will reveal that most mutant phenotypes, and most experimental outcomes, are scored by strong qualitative criteria. Similarly, many of the techniques used in molecular and cell biology (as opposed to, say, electrophysiology) do not give great insight into dynamic behavior. Many experiments depend on comparison between populations that have been perturbed more than a cell division ago (for example by a loss of function mutation in a gene) and control unperturbed populations, with dynamic behavior at best inferred by interpolation.

We cannot know in advance the extent to which observation of quantitative and dynamic behavior will uncover new phenomena and result in new understanding. However, we note that 20th century experience shows that the ability to define and score any phenotype leads to a deeper understanding of a process. For example, studies on cell lineage in C. elegans (Horvitz & Sulston, 1980) or the age of yeast mother cells as defined by the number of times that they have budded (Kennedy et al., 1994), has resulted in a deeper understanding of these processes. These include the isolation of new mutants, and the discovery of hitherto unknown mechanisms that control the processes. Similarly, many intracellular processes that are thermodynamically favored (e.g., protein folding, invasion of a negatively super-coiled double stranded DNA by a single homologous strand) turn out to be facilitated by gene products (proteins). As a further example, there is now some evidence (Wong et al., 1995, reviewed in Endy & Brent, 2001) that the pace of dynamic developmental events in polikothermic metazoans is under genetic control. Thus, based on historical experience, we expect that observations of quantitative and dynamic effects will define new phenotypes that will enable genetic studies from which deeper understanding will follow (see below).

c) The extent to which regulatory pathways work to amplify weak signals, or to produce all-or-nothing behaviors in response to threshold concentrations or in individual cells. This topic has been of interest to molecular biologists for some time (e.g., Ptashne, 1984). More recently, it has become interesting to non-biologists. A number of researchers (including one of the Center members, Bruck) have claimed MAP kinase signal transduction pathways function as switches, and that such behavior is important to the biology of the organisms that carries them (Huang & Ferrell, 1996; Ferrell & Bhatt, 1997; Ferrell & Machleder, 1998; Levchenko et al., 2000). Experiments at the Center can be expected to determine the extent of the various kinds of switch-like behavior(s) in this pathway, and to help characterize the identity and properties of the pathway elements that cause such behaviors to arise. Although it is premature to speculate on the consequences of such understanding for human health, a better understanding of this behavior would be of interest to biological engineers and designers of experimental biological computing architectures.
